title: AgeTech Concierge — AI care navigator for families with aging parents
tagline: The invisible layer that coordinates elder care so families don't burn out.
problem: When a parent turns 75+, managing their care becomes a second full-time job for adult children. Appointments, medications, insurance claims, home safety assessments, and social isolation all fall on family members with no coordination layer between them. There are hundreds of point solutions — telehealth apps, pill dispensers, fall sensors — but no orchestration layer that ties it all together.
solution: An AI-powered care concierge that acts as the command center for a senior's care. It ingests data from wearables, medical records, and family inputs, then proactively surfaces tasks: 'Your mum's next cardiology appointment is in 3 days — do you want me to arrange transport?' It handles insurance paperwork, medication reminders, and connects the family via a shared dashboard. Think Notion meets a geriatric care manager.
business_model: B2C subscription at $79/month per family. B2B2C via employer benefits (30M+ US adults are caregivers who lose productivity — employers pay for this). Enterprise tier for assisted living facilities to offer residents' families as a premium add-on.
why_now: 1 in 6 people globally will be 60+ by 2030. Less than 1% of VC goes to aging-focused startups. AI agent coordination tools are now mature enough to handle real workflow orchestration across health, logistics, and finance APIs.
moat: Data flywheel — the more care events it handles, the more predictive it becomes. Switching cost is high once a family's health history, insurance, and care network is inside the platform.
go_to_market: Partner with employer HR platforms (Workday, Rippling) to offer as a caregiving benefit. Target the 53 million unpaid caregivers in the US. Content marketing via guilt-free caregiving communities.
risks: Healthcare data regulation (HIPAA). Fragmented provider integrations. Requires trust in a sensitive domain — brand and tone are critical.
similar_companies: Honor (home care staffing), Papa (companion care), Homethrive (employer benefit), Wellthy (care concierge — closest comp, no AI layer)
founder_fit: Ideal for founders with healthcare, family caregiving, or enterprise HR benefits experience.

title: SubPay — The payment operating system for construction subcontractors
tagline: Turn 56-day waits into 48-hour payments without factoring fees.
market_size: $2.2 trillion US construction industry; $280B drained annually by payment delays
problem: Subcontractors in construction are the industry's invisible bankers. They complete work, pay for materials and labor upfront, then wait an average of 56 days to get paid — sometimes 90 to 120 days. The 2025 National Subcontractor Market Report found that 77% of subs cover material costs out of pocket while waiting, 43% lack working capital for unexpected expenses, and 1 in 3 draws from personal savings to cover cash deficits. The root cause is not just slow GCs — it is that pay application submission, approval tracking, lien waiver exchange, and financing are all handled in disconnected tools or by hand, which creates compounding delays at every step.
solution: SubPay is a mobile-first platform that gives subcontractors one place to submit pay applications correctly the first time, track approval status in real time, exchange lien waivers digitally, and unlock early payment on approved invoices instantly. When a pay app is approved by the GC, the sub can draw 90% of the invoice value within 24 hours at a transparent flat fee — no traditional factoring, no relationship disclosure to the GC. The platform integrates with Procore and Buildertrend on the GC side to pull approval signals automatically, eliminating the chase-and-follow-up cycle that adds weeks to payment timelines.
business_model: SaaS subscription at $99/month per subcontractor company for the pay application and compliance layer. Early payment fee of 1.5-2.5% per 30-day term on drawn invoices — revenue scales with the subcontractor's invoice volume. Target average invoice size of $45K means each draw generates $675-$1,125 in fee revenue. Enterprise tier for trade associations and specialty contractors managing multiple crews at $299/month.
why_now: Mechanic's lien filings surged 141% in 2023 as payment conditions worsened — subcontractors are desperate for alternatives. GC platforms like Procore and Buildertrend now offer open APIs, making payment status data accessible programmatically for the first time. The embedded finance infrastructure (Banking-as-a-Service rails) has matured to where a small team can offer instant payouts without a banking license. Billd raised capital proving the demand exists, but left the workflow layer entirely unsolved.
moat: Network effects as GCs are onboarded — the more GCs that integrate, the faster approval signals flow and the lower the early payment risk. Proprietary payment history data on GC behaviour creates a unique credit model for subcontractors that traditional lenders cannot replicate. Switching cost compounds as compliance documents, lien waiver history, and pay app records accumulate on the platform.
go_to_market: Launch with a single trade vertical — electrical subcontractors — in one metro market (Dallas or Phoenix, both high-growth construction markets). Partner with NECA (National Electrical Contractors Association) and local trade chapters for distribution. Offer the compliance layer free for 90 days to drive adoption, then monetise on early payment draws. Referral program where subs who bring their GC onto the integration earn a rate discount.
risks: Lending license requirements vary by state and may require partnerships with bank sponsors. GC platform integrations depend on API access that could be restricted. Credit risk on early payment draws if GC does not ultimately pay. Competition from Procore and Buildertrend adding native payment features.
similar_companies: Billd (material financing for subs — does not solve the pay app workflow layer), Constrafor (GC-side procurement and compliance), Levelset (lien management only, acquired by Procore 2022), Payra (GC-side AR automation — opposite side of the relationship)
founder_fit: Ideal for founders with construction industry operations experience combined with fintech or lending background. A technical co-founder familiar with financial APIs and BaaS infrastructure is essential.

title: SupplierCarbon — Scope 3 compliance tool built for suppliers, not buyers
tagline: Stop losing enterprise contracts because you can't answer a carbon data request.
market_size: $2.5 billion Scope 3 supplier data platforms market in 2024, growing to $8.7B by 2033 at 15% CAGR
problem: In 2025, over 70,000 mid-market suppliers received formal requests from enterprise customers to disclose their carbon emissions data via CDP — and 64% did not respond. The reason is not indifference: it is that every existing tool (Watershed, Persefoni, Sweep, EcoVadis) is priced for the Fortune 500 buyer at $180,000-$280,000 per year, leaving the supplier with nothing affordable or purpose-built. Meanwhile, California SB 253 requires Scope 3 data collection to begin in 2026 for 2027 reporting, and CSRD is pulling European supply chain disclosure obligations down to SME suppliers. A mid-market manufacturer that fails to respond to an enterprise customer's CDP or CSRD data request increasingly risks losing the contract — yet the average sustainability consultant charges $15,000-$50,000 just to file a single report.
solution: SupplierCarbon is a $149/month SaaS platform built specifically for the supplier side of Scope 3. It guides a company through measuring its own Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions (which become the Scope 3 data their customers need), packages the output in every required format — CDP questionnaire, CSRD ESRS data points, custom buyer portals — and stores it so the same data can be reused across multiple customer requests with one click. When a new data request arrives from a customer, the supplier logs in, selects the requesting customer's framework, reviews the pre-populated data, and submits in under 20 minutes. The platform also benchmarks the supplier's emissions against industry peers, identifies the top three reduction opportunities, and generates a plain-English summary the supplier can share proactively with their sales team to turn compliance into a competitive advantage.
business_model: SaaS subscription at $149/month (billed annually at $1,490) for companies with up to 500 employees. Mid-tier at $299/month for 500-2,000 employees. One-time data verification add-on at $499 for third-party limited assurance — required for California SB 253 and increasingly demanded by enterprise buyers. Distribution partnership revenue share with procurement platforms and trade associations who embed SupplierCarbon as a compliance module for their supplier networks.
why_now: California SB 253 mandates Scope 3 data collection starting in 2026 for reporting in 2027, creating a hard compliance deadline for thousands of US suppliers. In 2025, 70,000+ suppliers received CDP data requests — a 3x increase from five years prior — and only 36% responded, signalling massive unmet demand. The EU Omnibus directive in February 2026 narrowed CSRD scope but did not remove the obligation for large companies, who continue to push requirements down to their supply chains. Enterprise procurement teams are now explicitly scoring supplier responsiveness on sustainability data as a vendor qualification criterion.
moat: Reuse flywheel — every hour a supplier invests in entering their emissions data makes the platform stickier, as that data becomes reusable across all future customer requests. Multi-framework output means a supplier who sets up once can serve CDP, CSRD, ISSB, and custom buyer portals without re-entering data. Supplier network data creates an anonymised benchmarking database that becomes more accurate and valuable as the customer base grows — a defensible asset no newcomer can replicate quickly.
go_to_market: Target mid-market US manufacturers in California first — they face the earliest hard deadline under SB 253. Partner with the National Association of Manufacturers (NAM) and California Manufacturers and Technology Association (CMTA) to reach members en masse. Offer a free Scope 1 and 2 emissions baseline calculator as a lead magnet — users who complete it see their gap versus what their customers will request, creating natural conversion. Sales motion: identify suppliers who received a CDP request in 2025 but did not respond (CDP publishes this data publicly) and reach out directly with a 14-day free trial.
risks: Regulatory uncertainty — the GHG Protocol Scope 3 Standard is being revised with a public draft expected late 2026, which could require platform updates. Enterprise buyers may build supplier-facing portals themselves, reducing the need for an independent tool. Scope of emissions calculation requires sector-specific emission factors that must be kept current. Competing on price with free-tier offerings from incumbent platforms like EcoVadis.
similar_companies: Watershed (enterprise buyer-side platform, $180K+/year — no supplier-focused product), Persefoni (enterprise carbon accounting, not supplier-optimised), Ecochain (lifecycle assessment tool for product carbon footprints — adjacent but more technical), Hedgehog Carbon (small UK startup serving European suppliers — no US market presence or multi-framework output)
founder_fit: Ideal for founders with sustainability consulting, enterprise SaaS sales, or supply chain management backgrounds. Deep familiarity with CDP questionnaire structure and GHG Protocol methodology is a meaningful advantage. A regulatory-literate technical co-founder who can build framework output adapters quickly is essential as the standard landscape shifts.

title: PathwayOS — The missing operating system for Career and Technical Education directors
tagline: CTE programs serve 11 million students with no purpose-built software to run them.
market_size: $1.4 billion in annual federal Perkins V funding; 11.2 million students enrolled across 30,000+ US CTE programs
problem: Career and Technical Education enrollment surged 10% in a single year — from 7.8 million to 8.6 million students between 2022-23 and 2023-24, and now exceeds 11.2 million nationwide. CTE directors are responsible for managing industry partnerships, tracking student credential attainment, demonstrating Perkins V federal funding compliance, and connecting graduating students with employers — all while running programs across automotive, healthcare, construction, IT, and culinary trades simultaneously. Yet 40% of CTE programs report struggling to maintain employer partnerships, 66% say students lack awareness of available pathways, and directors universally manage these complex operations through a combination of spreadsheets, generic school information systems built for academic subjects, and manual email chains. The EdTech market has produced hundreds of tools for academic instruction but almost nothing built specifically for the operational reality of a CTE program director.
solution: PathwayOS is a purpose-built SaaS platform for CTE program directors that unifies three critical workflows in one place. First, an employer partnership CRM that tracks every industry partner relationship — MOU status, work-based learning placements, guest speaker history, and advisory board participation — replacing the spreadsheets that currently fall apart when a director changes roles. Second, a student outcome tracker that follows each student from enrollment through credential attainment to employment, generating the Perkins V Consolidated Annual Report data automatically rather than requiring hundreds of manual hours at year-end. Third, a student-facing career pathway explorer that lets students browse program options, see local employer partners, and apply for work-based learning placements — addressing the awareness gap that 66% of CTE directors cite as their top challenge. The platform is district-licensed, meaning the data persists across staff turnover and scales across multiple program areas without reconfiguration.
business_model: District SaaS license at $8,000-$18,000 per year depending on number of CTE program pathways and student enrollment. This positions PathwayOS squarely within Perkins V local allocation spending — districts receive formula-based Perkins funding specifically to improve program quality, and software that automates compliance reporting is a direct and defensible use of these funds. State-level licenses for state CTE directors who want aggregated outcome data across all districts. Professional services revenue from Perkins V state plan alignment consulting for districts transitioning from manual reporting.
why_now: CTE enrollment has grown 10% year-over-year as employers and policymakers push vocational pathways as an alternative to four-year degrees. The Trump administration's 2026 federal budget proposes consolidating education programs and increasing state and local control — meaning districts that can demonstrate outcomes with data will protect their funding, while those that cannot may see cuts. Holon IQ's 2026 Global Education Outlook confirms workforce training attracted the most M&A activity in 2025, signalling rising investor and acquirer interest in the space. No purpose-built CTE operations platform exists at scale — the closest competitors are generic school administration tools that require heavy customisation.
moat: Institutional switching cost — once a district's employer partnerships, student outcome history, and Perkins compliance records live in PathwayOS, migration risk is extremely high, especially given staff turnover. Network effect within states: when multiple districts in a state use PathwayOS, the state CTE director gains an aggregated view that creates pressure for non-adopting districts to join. Perkins V reporting automation becomes more accurate over time as the platform learns district-specific credential and outcome mapping.
go_to_market: Launch with a single state — target Texas or California, both of which have large CTE enrollments and strong state-level CTE advocacy organisations. Partner with Advance CTE (the national membership organisation for state CTE directors) and the Association for Career and Technical Education (ACTE) to reach program directors. Offer a free Perkins V CAR report generator as a standalone tool — any director who uses it to save 200+ hours of year-end reporting work becomes a natural conversion target for the full platform. Attend SkillsUSA and ACTE National Policy Seminar to build relationships with early adopters.
risks: School district sales cycles are long — typically 6-18 months with procurement committee review. Perkins V funding allocation rules vary by state and may restrict how funds can be used for software. Staff turnover at the director level is high, meaning champions may leave mid-sale. Generic SIS vendors (PowerSchool, Infinite Campus) could add CTE-specific modules to their existing district relationships.
similar_companies: Xello (career exploration platform for K-12 — student-facing only, no director operations layer), YouScience (aptitude and career matching — assessment tool, not program management), Naviance (college and career readiness — higher ed focused, not CTE operations), Kuder (career planning platform — no employer partnership or Perkins compliance layer)
founder_fit: Ideal for founders who have worked inside a school district, state education agency, or CTE advocacy organisation and understand the Perkins V compliance burden firsthand. A technical co-founder who can build a clean, non-intimidating interface for non-technical program directors is essential — this is not a product that can afford enterprise UX complexity.
